Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} years ({{ill|lt=810 years|Q23819|WD=Q23819}}-{{ill|lt=824 years|Q30353|WD=Q30353}}) |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Gongenzo|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268}} |Other names = Samukawa Shrine, Miyama Myojin, Ninomiya Myojin|Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963}} |Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Shimosa Sanzan Seven Year Festival|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Ninomiya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Torii]]'''Ninomiya Shrine'' (Ninomiya Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011}}{{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417}} Miyama. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}, tell them {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. In ancient times, it was also called ``Miyama Myojin'' or ``Ninomiya Myojin''. . === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is listed according to the shrine history book. * '''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Kenhaya Susano no Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Kushinada Hime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushiinada Hime no Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira Mikoto}}''' :According to legend, when {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Moritsune|Q11623227|WD=Q11623227}}, who was demoted in 4th year ({{ill|lt=1180|Q19702|WD=Q19702}}), settled in this area, he enshrined his ancestor, Fujiwara Tokihira, in his side hall. Since Tokihira is a political enemy of {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} (Tenjin), it is said that the parishioners of Tenjin did not visit the shrine . * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Daisaku no Mikoto}}''' :{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q3125096}} Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, a nearby mukakusha, was {{ill|lt=together enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondabetsu no Mikoto}}''' :In the Meiji period, Setsusha and Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. === History === According to the shrine's legend, the shrine history book was founded by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654}} during the {{ill|lt=Konin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} years ({{ill|lt=810|Q23819|WD=Q23819}} to {{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353}}). . Like place names, the name ``Miyama'' appeared to be ``Miyama'' in ancient times, and was attributed to our company. {{ill|lt=Miyama clan|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183}} served as {{ill|lt=priests|Q11589454|WD=Q11589454}} for generations. ``Chiba Prefecture place name'' Miyama Village. .``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho}} states: Samukawa Shrine", but our company is said to be {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}}. It is also said that it was revered by the imperial court as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695}} (however, {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538}} of {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868}} is also called Ninomiya). It is unknown when it began to be called Ninomiya Shrine, but (Japan)|Q1327472|Inuigen}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1303|Q5614|WD=Q5614}}) The temple bell (stored in Shohoin, Narita City) says ``Soshu Ninomiya Shadan'', which suggests that this name already existed in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} In 19 years ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}} gave {{ill|lt=shuinji/black seals|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinji}} 10 koku and , {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662}}The shogun made donations many times. {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}}It is introduced in ``Narita Pilgrimage Record'' published in 1858 along with an illustration of ``Miyama Myo Shrine''. After entering {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In 1910, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Gensetsusha Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. Since ancient times, "Shirenshita" (area of parishioners) has consisted of 21.3 villages (currently {{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417}} eastern and northern areas, {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047}}, {{ja link|Yachiyo City|Q328651}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616}}), and its influence can still be seen today at annual festivals. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The current shrine building was rebuilt during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} years ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|WD=Q7690}} to {{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|WD=Q7719}}). Both the main shrine and worship hall have copper plate roofs, but the main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}11 ({{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165}})1 In October, the worship hall had its {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531}} roof replaced in Taisho 14 ({{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107}}). The floor plan of this shrine is {{ill|lt=Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268}}, which was popular during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, but the floor height from {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} to {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699}} is the same. In addition, the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q11637472|WD=Q11637472}}'s {{ill|lt=kumimono|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068}} and koshigumi were designed and constructed using the regular {{ill|lt=miya carpenter|Q65261787|WD=Q65261787}} method of the time. In 1978 ({{ill|lt=1978|Q2483|WD=Q2483}}) {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954}}, it was designated as {{ill|lt=List of Funabashi City Designated Cultural Properties|Q11614137|WD=Q11614137|lt=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties}}. File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) haiden.JPG|Shrine of worshipFile:ninomiyajinnjya7.jpg|Mae Square File: Ninomiya.jpg|Photo of the shrine building in 1918 === Approach === [[File:Ninomiya-Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Sando]] When you approach the shrine from the torii gate in front of the shrine, the approach becomes lower and then crosses the valley. Furthermore, a stream flows at the bottom of the valley, and the water vein is said to lead to a pond within the grounds of {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900}}, which is enshrined in {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma|Q7849928|WD=Q7849928}} in {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047}}. If you walk along the valley floor in a straight line, by the time you reach the top of the stone steps, you will be able to see the Karahafu facing the worship hall, and the master carpenter's {{ill|lt=Design|Q82604|WD=Q82604}} has been ingenious so that you can take your time to admire the large, majestic {{ill|lt=Karagohafu|Q977293|WD=Q977293}} curves and the majestic carvings. === Festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} (January 1st) * Oshahatsu Festival (January 7th) * Otsutsu porridge cooking ritual (January 14th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Festival}} (February 3rd) * '''Regular Festival''' (October 16th) * '''Daikaage'' (Obiyo, {{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066}}night of November 13th) :A festival named after the bonfires lit by villagers to welcome Fujiwara no Moritsune and others who were said to have been exiled to this area. * '''{{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280}}''' (Year of the Ox/Year of the Wild) :A festival held once every seven years, centered around our company, with nine shrines in Funabashi City, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616}}, {{ill|lt=Yachiyo City|Q328651|WD=Q328651}}, and Narashino City participating. Our company plays the role of "father." === Surroundings === * '''{{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple (Funabashi City)|Q11589481|WD=Q11589481|lt=Douzan Jinguji Temple}}''' : Adjacent. Our company {{ill|lt=Bettoji Temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} before {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. === Cultural assets === === Chiba Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280}} - Designated on March 30, 2004. === Cultural property designated by Funabashi City === * Shrine building (tangible cultural property (building)) - Designated on February 27, 1992. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saito Sonojo|1=UNKNOWN}} etc. dedicated haiku plaque (tangible cultural property (historical material)) - Designated on October 23, 1980. * Ninomiya Shrine Kagura (Intangible Folk Cultural Property) - Designated on June 26, 1995. * Ginkgo at Ninomiya Shrine (natural monument) - Designated on March 30, 2012. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma Station|Q801534|WD=Q801534}} ({{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}}{{ill|lt=Sobu Main Line|Q1191364|WD=Q1191364}}) From the north exit ** Take the {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya Shrine" (ride time: 20 minutes) ** Take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya-jinja-mae" (20 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Yakuendai Station|Q8047226|WD=Q8047226}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Matsudo Line|Q3553734|WD=Q3553734}}) ** Walk (3 minutes) from the Yakuendai Station Iriguchi bus stop on National Route 296, take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya-jinja-mae (10 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Keisei Okubo Station|Q6383851|WD=Q6383851}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Main Line|Q1063675|WD=Q1063675}}) ** Walk (7 minutes) from the Toho Daigaku-mae bus stop, take {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya Shrine (ride time: 6 minutes) *: Keisei buses near Keisei Okubo Station have different routes for going down and going up. The nearest bus stop for going down is "Toho Daigaku-mae" and the nearest bus stop for going up is "Okubo Jujiro". === Source === {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * "Japanese Historical Place Name System Chiba Prefecture Place Names" ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}) Funabashi City Ninomiya Shrine Section * “Meiji, Taisho, and Showa eras preserved in postcard photographs” (Funabashi City Local Museum, March 23, 2005) * “Celebrating the 70th Anniversary of the Municipal System - 100 Years of Funabashi” (Kyoto Publishing, February 5, 2007) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Mr. Miyama|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183}} * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280}} * {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900}} * {{ill|lt=Samukawa Shrine (Chiba City)|Q11457393|WD=Q11457393|lt=Samukawa Shrine}} (Chiba Prefecture {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616}}{{ill|lt=Chuo Ward (Chiba City)|Q1075869|WD=Q1075869|lt=Chuo Ward}}) - Like our company, Shikinai Shrine and Samukawa Shrine Ronsha * {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538}} (Chiba Prefecture {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868}}) - Another shrine named Shimousa Province Ninomiya * {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}} === External link === {{commonscat|Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi)}} * [http://www.ninomiya-jinja.jp Ninomiya Shrine] (official website) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ninomiya Shinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:船橋市の神社]] [[Category:下総国の式内社]] [[Category:千葉県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下総国|社2]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:嵯峨天皇]] [[Category:藤原時平]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QNinomiya Shrine (Q11371511)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QNinomiya Shrine (Q11371511)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)}}[[Category:qq]]

Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810 years|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}}-{{ill|lt=824 years|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}) |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Gongenzo|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} |Other names = Samukawa Shrine, Miyama Myojin, Ninomiya Myojin|Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} |Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Shimosa Sanzan Seven Year Festival|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Ninomiya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Torii]]'''Ninomiya Shrine'' (Ninomiya Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} Miyama. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, tell them {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In ancient times, it was also called ``Miyama Myojin'' or ``Ninomiya Myojin''. . === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is listed according to the shrine history book. * '''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Kenhaya Susano no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Kushinada Hime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushiinada Hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira Mikoto|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}''' :According to legend, when {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Moritsune|Q11623227|WD=Q11623227|1=Fujiwara no Moritsune}}, who was demoted in 4th year ({{ill|lt=1180|Q19702|WD=Q19702|1=1180}}), settled in this area, he enshrined his ancestor, Fujiwara Tokihira, in his side hall. Since Tokihira is a political enemy of {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (Tenjin), it is said that the parishioners of Tenjin did not visit the shrine . * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Daisaku no Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}}''' :{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, a nearby mukakusha, was {{ill|lt=together enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondabetsu no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}''' :In the Meiji period, Setsusha and Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. === History === According to the shrine's legend, the shrine history book was founded by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} during the {{ill|lt=Konin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}} to {{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}). . Like place names, the name ``Miyama'' appeared to be ``Miyama'' in ancient times, and was attributed to our company. {{ill|lt=Miyama clan|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Miyama clan}} served as {{ill|lt=priests|Q11589454|WD=Q11589454|1=Shinkan (official)}} for generations. ``Chiba Prefecture place name'' Miyama Village. .``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} states: Samukawa Shrine", but our company is said to be {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. It is also said that it was revered by the imperial court as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} (however, {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}} is also called Ninomiya). It is unknown when it began to be called Ninomiya Shrine, but (Japan)|Q1327472|Inuigen}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1303|Q5614|WD=Q5614|1=1303}}) The temple bell (stored in Shohoin, Narita City) says ``Soshu Ninomiya Shadan'', which suggests that this name already existed in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} In 19 years ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} gave {{ill|lt=shuinji/black seals|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinji|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} 10 koku and , {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}The shogun made donations many times. {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}}It is introduced in ``Narita Pilgrimage Record'' published in 1858 along with an illustration of ``Miyama Myo Shrine''. After entering {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In 1910, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Gensetsusha Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. Since ancient times, "Shirenshita" (area of parishioners) has consisted of 21.3 villages (currently {{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} eastern and northern areas, {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}, {{ja link|Yachiyo City|Q328651}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}), and its influence can still be seen today at annual festivals. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The current shrine building was rebuilt during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} years ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|WD=Q7690|1=1772}} to {{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|WD=Q7719|1=1781}}). Both the main shrine and worship hall have copper plate roofs, but the main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}11 ({{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}})1 In October, the worship hall had its {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531|1=Thatching}} roof replaced in Taisho 14 ({{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}}). The floor plan of this shrine is {{ill|lt=Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}}, which was popular during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but the floor height from {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} to {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is the same. In addition, the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q11637472|WD=Q11637472|1=eaves}}'s {{ill|lt=kumimono|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} and koshigumi were designed and constructed using the regular {{ill|lt=miya carpenter|Q65261787|WD=Q65261787|1=miyadaiku}} method of the time. In 1978 ({{ill|lt=1978|Q2483|WD=Q2483|1=1978}}) {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, it was designated as {{ill|lt=List of Funabashi City Designated Cultural Properties|Q11614137|WD=Q11614137|lt=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties|1=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties}}. File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) haiden.JPG|Shrine of worshipFile:ninomiyajinnjya7.jpg|Mae Square File: Ninomiya.jpg|Photo of the shrine building in 1918 === Approach === [[File:Ninomiya-Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Sando]] When you approach the shrine from the torii gate in front of the shrine, the approach becomes lower and then crosses the valley. Furthermore, a stream flows at the bottom of the valley, and the water vein is said to lead to a pond within the grounds of {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}}, which is enshrined in {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma|Q7849928|WD=Q7849928|1=Tsudanuma}} in {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}. If you walk along the valley floor in a straight line, by the time you reach the top of the stone steps, you will be able to see the Karahafu facing the worship hall, and the master carpenter's {{ill|lt=Design|Q82604|WD=Q82604|1=Design}} has been ingenious so that you can take your time to admire the large, majestic {{ill|lt=Karagohafu|Q977293|WD=Q977293|1=Karahafu}} curves and the majestic carvings. === Festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} (January 1st) * Oshahatsu Festival (January 7th) * Otsutsu porridge cooking ritual (January 14th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Festival|1=Setsubun}} (February 3rd) * '''Regular Festival''' (October 16th) * '''Daikaage'' (Obiyo, {{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}night of November 13th) :A festival named after the bonfires lit by villagers to welcome Fujiwara no Moritsune and others who were said to have been exiled to this area. * '''{{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}}''' (Year of the Ox/Year of the Wild) :A festival held once every seven years, centered around our company, with nine shrines in Funabashi City, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yachiyo City|Q328651|WD=Q328651|1=Yachiyo, Chiba}}, and Narashino City participating. Our company plays the role of "father." === Surroundings === * '''{{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple (Funabashi City)|Q11589481|WD=Q11589481|lt=Douzan Jinguji Temple|1=Jingū-ji}}''' : Adjacent. Our company {{ill|lt=Bettoji Temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} before {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. === Cultural assets === === Chiba Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} - Designated on March 30, 2004. === Cultural property designated by Funabashi City === * Shrine building (tangible cultural property (building)) - Designated on February 27, 1992. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saito Sonojo|1=UNKNOWN}} etc. dedicated haiku plaque (tangible cultural property (historical material)) - Designated on October 23, 1980. * Ninomiya Shrine Kagura (Intangible Folk Cultural Property) - Designated on June 26, 1995. * Ginkgo at Ninomiya Shrine (natural monument) - Designated on March 30, 2012. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma Station|Q801534|WD=Q801534|1=Tsudanuma Station}} ({{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|lt=Sobu Main Line|Q1191364|WD=Q1191364|1=Sōbu Main Line}}) From the north exit ** Take the {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya Shrine" (ride time: 20 minutes) ** Take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya-jinja-mae" (20 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Yakuendai Station|Q8047226|WD=Q8047226|1=Yakuendai Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Matsudo Line|Q3553734|WD=Q3553734|1=Keisei Matsudo Line}}) ** Walk (3 minutes) from the Yakuendai Station Iriguchi bus stop on National Route 296, take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya-jinja-mae (10 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Keisei Okubo Station|Q6383851|WD=Q6383851|1=Keisei Ōkubo Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Main Line|Q1063675|WD=Q1063675|1=Keisei Main Line}}) ** Walk (7 minutes) from the Toho Daigaku-mae bus stop, take {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya Shrine (ride time: 6 minutes) *: Keisei buses near Keisei Okubo Station have different routes for going down and going up. The nearest bus stop for going down is "Toho Daigaku-mae" and the nearest bus stop for going up is "Okubo Jujiro". === Source === {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * "Japanese Historical Place Name System Chiba Prefecture Place Names" ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}) Funabashi City Ninomiya Shrine Section * “Meiji, Taisho, and Showa eras preserved in postcard photographs” (Funabashi City Local Museum, March 23, 2005) * “Celebrating the 70th Anniversary of the Municipal System - In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, tell them {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In ancient times, it was also called ``Miyama Myojin'' or ``Ninomiya Myojin''. . === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is listed according to the shrine history book. * '''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Kenhaya Susano no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Kushinada Hime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushiinada Hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira Mikoto|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}''' :According to legend, when {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Moritsune|Q11623227|WD=Q11623227|1=Fujiwara no Moritsune}}, who was demoted in 4th year ({{ill|lt=1180|Q19702|WD=Q19702|1=1180}}), settled in this area, he enshrined his ancestor, Fujiwara Tokihira, in his side hall. Since Tokihira is a political enemy of {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (Tenjin), it is said that the parishioners of Tenjin did not visit the shrine . * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Daisaku no Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}}''' :{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, a nearby mukakusha, was {{ill|lt=together enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondabetsu no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}''' :In the Meiji period, Setsusha and Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. === History === According to the shrine's legend, the shrine history book was founded by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} during the {{ill|lt=Konin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}} to {{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}). . Like place names, the name ``Miyama'' appeared to be ``Miyama'' in ancient times, and was attributed to our company. {{ill|lt=Miyama clan|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Miyama clan}} served as {{ill|lt=priests|Q11589454|WD=Q11589454|1=Shinkan (official)}} for generations. ``Chiba Prefecture place name'' Miyama Village. .``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} states: Samukawa Shrine", but our company is said to be {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. It is also said that it was revered by the imperial court as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} (however, {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}} is also called Ninomiya). It is unknown when it began to be called Ninomiya Shrine, but (Japan)|Q1327472|Inuigen}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1303|Q5614|WD=Q5614|1=1303}}) The temple bell (stored in Shohoin, Narita City) says ``Soshu Ninomiya Shadan'', which suggests that this name already existed in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} In 19 years ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} gave {{ill|lt=shuinji/black seals|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinji|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} 10 koku and , {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}The shogun made donations many times. {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}}It is introduced in ``Narita Pilgrimage Record'' published in 1858 along with an illustration of ``Miyama Myo Shrine''. After entering {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In 1910, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Gensetsusha Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. Since ancient times, "Shirenshita" (area of parishioners) has consisted of 21.3 villages (currently {{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} eastern and northern areas, {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}, {{ja link|Yachiyo City|Q328651}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}), and its influence can still be seen today at annual festivals. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The current shrine building was rebuilt during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} years ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|WD=Q7690|1=1772}} to {{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|WD=Q7719|1=1781}}). Both the main shrine and worship hall have copper plate roofs, but the main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}11 ({{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}})1 In October, the worship hall had its {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531|1=Thatching}} roof replaced in Taisho 14 ({{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}}). The floor plan of this shrine is {{ill|lt=Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}}, which was popular during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but the floor height from {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} to {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is the same. In addition, the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q11637472|WD=Q11637472|1=eaves}}'s {{ill|lt=kumimono|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} and koshigumi were designed and constructed using the regular {{ill|lt=miya carpenter|Q65261787|WD=Q65261787|1=miyadaiku}} method of the time. In 1978 ({{ill|lt=1978|Q2483|WD=Q2483|1=1978}}) {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, it was designated as {{ill|lt=List of Funabashi City Designated Cultural Properties|Q11614137|WD=Q11614137|lt=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties|1=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties}}. File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) haiden.JPG|Shrine of worshipFile:ninomiyajinnjya7.jpg|Mae Square File: Ninomiya.jpg|Photo of the shrine building in 1918 === Approach === [[File:Ninomiya-Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Sando]] When you approach the shrine from the torii gate in front of the shrine, the approach becomes lower and then crosses the valley. Furthermore, a stream flows at the bottom of the valley, and the water vein is said to lead to a pond within the grounds of {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}}, which is enshrined in {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma|Q7849928|WD=Q7849928|1=Tsudanuma}} in {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}. If you walk along the valley floor in a straight line, by the time you reach the top of the stone steps, you will be able to see the Karahafu facing the worship hall, and the master carpenter's {{ill|lt=Design|Q82604|WD=Q82604|1=Design}} has been ingenious so that you can take your time to admire the large, majestic {{ill|lt=Karagohafu|Q977293|WD=Q977293|1=Karahafu}} curves and the majestic carvings. === Festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} (January 1st) * Oshahatsu Festival (January 7th) * Otsutsu porridge cooking ritual (January 14th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Festival|1=Setsubun}} (February 3rd) * '''Regular Festival''' (October 16th) * '''Daikaage'' (Obiyo, {{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}night of November 13th) :A festival named after the bonfires lit by villagers to welcome Fujiwara no Moritsune and others who were said to have been exiled to this area. * '''{{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}}''' (Year of the Ox/Year of the Wild) :A festival held once every seven years, centered around our company, with nine shrines in Funabashi City, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yachiyo City|Q328651|WD=Q328651|1=Yachiyo, Chiba}}, and Narashino City participating. Our company plays the role of "father." === Surroundings === * '''{{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple (Funabashi City)|Q11589481|WD=Q11589481|lt=Douzan Jinguji Temple|1=Jingū-ji}}''' : Adjacent. Our company {{ill|lt=Bettoji Temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} before {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. === Cultural assets === === Chiba Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} - Designated on March 30, 2004. === Cultural property designated by Funabashi City === * Shrine building (tangible cultural property (building)) - Designated on February 27, 1992. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saito Sonojo|1=UNKNOWN}} etc. dedicated haiku plaque (tangible cultural property (historical material)) - Designated on October 23, 1980. * Ninomiya Shrine Kagura (Intangible Folk Cultural Property) - Designated on June 26, 1995. * Ginkgo at Ninomiya Shrine (natural monument) - Designated on March 30, 2012. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma Station|Q801534|WD=Q801534|1=Tsudanuma Station}} ({{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|lt=Sobu Main Line|Q1191364|WD=Q1191364|1=Sōbu Main Line}}) From the north exit ** Take the {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya Shrine" (ride time: 20 minutes) ** Take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya-jinja-mae" (20 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Yakuendai Station|Q8047226|WD=Q8047226|1=Yakuendai Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Matsudo Line|Q3553734|WD=Q3553734|1=Keisei Matsudo Line}}) ** Walk (3 minutes) from the Yakuendai Station Iriguchi bus stop on National Route 296, take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya-jinja-mae (10 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Keisei Okubo Station|Q6383851|WD=Q6383851|1=Keisei Ōkubo Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Main Line|Q1063675|WD=Q1063675|1=Keisei Main Line}}) ** Walk (7 minutes) from the Toho Daigaku-mae bus stop, take {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya Shrine (ride time: 6 minutes) *: Keisei buses near Keisei Okubo Station have different routes for going down and going up. The nearest bus stop for going down is "Toho Daigaku-mae" and the nearest bus stop for going up is "Okubo Jujiro". === Source === {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * "Japanese Historical Place Name System Chiba Prefecture Place Names" ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}) Funabashi City Ninomiya Shrine Section * “Meiji, Taisho, and Showa eras preserved in postcard photographs” (Funabashi City Local Museum, March 23, 2005) * “Celebrating the 70th Anniversary of the Municipal System - Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810 years|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}}-{{ill|lt=824 years|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}) |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Gongenzo|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} |Other names = Samukawa Shrine, Miyama Myojin, Ninomiya Myojin|Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} |Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Shimosa Sanzan Seven Year Festival|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Ninomiya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Torii]]'''Ninomiya Shrine'' (Ninomiya Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} Miyama. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, tell them {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In ancient times, it was also called ``Miyama Myojin'' or ``Ninomiya Myojin''. . === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is listed according to the shrine history book. * '''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Kenhaya Susano no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Kushinada Hime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushiinada Hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira Mikoto|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}''' :According to legend, when {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Moritsune|Q11623227|WD=Q11623227|1=Fujiwara no Moritsune}}, who was demoted in 4th year ({{ill|lt=1180|Q19702|WD=Q19702|1=1180}}), settled in this area, he enshrined his ancestor, Fujiwara Tokihira, in his side hall. Since Tokihira is a political enemy of {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (Tenjin), it is said that the parishioners of Tenjin did not visit the shrine . * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Daisaku no Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}}''' :{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, a nearby mukakusha, was {{ill|lt=together enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondabetsu no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}''' :In the Meiji period, Setsusha and Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. === History === According to the shrine's legend, the shrine history book was founded by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} during the {{ill|lt=Konin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}} to {{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}). . Like place names, the name ``Miyama'' appeared to be ``Miyama'' in ancient times, and was attributed to our company. {{ill|lt=Miyama clan|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Miyama clan}} served as {{ill|lt=priests|Q11589454|WD=Q11589454|1=Shinkan (official)}} for generations. ``Chiba Prefecture place name'' Miyama Village. .``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} states: Samukawa Shrine", but our company is said to be {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. It is also said that it was revered by the imperial court as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} (however, {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}} is also called Ninomiya). It is unknown when it began to be called Ninomiya Shrine, but (Japan)|Q1327472|Inuigen}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1303|Q5614|WD=Q5614|1=1303}}) The temple bell (stored in Shohoin, Narita City) says ``Soshu Ninomiya Shadan'', which suggests that this name already existed in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} In 19 years ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} gave {{ill|lt=shuinji/black seals|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinji|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} 10 koku and , {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}The shogun made donations many times. {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}}It is introduced in ``Narita Pilgrimage Record'' published in 1858 along with an illustration of ``Miyama Myo Shrine''. After entering {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In 1910, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Gensetsusha Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. Since ancient times, "Shirenshita" (area of parishioners) has consisted of 21.3 villages (currently {{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} eastern and northern areas, {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}, {{ja link|Yachiyo City|Q328651}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}), and its influence can still be seen today at annual festivals. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The current shrine building was rebuilt during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} years ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|WD=Q7690|1=1772}} to {{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|WD=Q7719|1=1781}}). Both the main shrine and worship hall have copper plate roofs, but the main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}11 ({{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}})1 In October, the worship hall had its {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531|1=Thatching}} roof replaced in Taisho 14 ({{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}}). The floor plan of this shrine is {{ill|lt=Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}}, which was popular during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but the floor height from {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} to {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is the same. In addition, the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q11637472|WD=Q11637472|1=eaves}}'s {{ill|lt=kumimono|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} and koshigumi were designed and constructed using the regular {{ill|lt=miya carpenter|Q65261787|WD=Q65261787|1=miyadaiku}} method of the time. In 1978 ({{ill|lt=1978|Q2483|WD=Q2483|1=1978}}) {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, it was designated as {{ill|lt=List of Funabashi City Designated Cultural Properties|Q11614137|WD=Q11614137|lt=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties|1=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties}}. File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) haiden.JPG|Shrine of worshipFile:ninomiyajinnjya7.jpg|Mae Square File: Ninomiya.jpg|Photo of the shrine building in 1918 === Approach === [[File:Ninomiya-Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Sando]] When you approach the shrine from the torii gate in front of the shrine, the approach becomes lower and then crosses the valley. Furthermore, a stream flows at the bottom of the valley, and the water vein is said to lead to a pond within the grounds of {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}}, which is enshrined in {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma|Q7849928|WD=Q7849928|1=Tsudanuma}} in {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}. If you walk along the valley floor in a straight line, by the time you reach the top of the stone steps, you will be able to see the Karahafu facing the worship hall, and the master carpenter's {{ill|lt=Design|Q82604|WD=Q82604|1=Design}} has been ingenious so that you can take your time to admire the large, majestic {{ill|lt=Karagohafu|Q977293|WD=Q977293|1=Karahafu}} curves and the majestic carvings. === Festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} (January 1st) * Oshahatsu Festival (January 7th) * Otsutsu porridge cooking ritual (January 14th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Festival|1=Setsubun}} (February 3rd) * '''Regular Festival''' (October 16th) * '''Daikaage'' (Obiyo, {{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}night of November 13th) :A festival named after the bonfires lit by villagers to welcome Fujiwara no Moritsune and others who were said to have been exiled to this area. * '''{{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}}''' (Year of the Ox/Year of the Wild) :A festival held once every seven years, centered around our company, with nine shrines in Funabashi City, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yachiyo City|Q328651|WD=Q328651|1=Yachiyo, Chiba}}, and Narashino City participating. Our company plays the role of "father." === Surroundings === * '''{{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple (Funabashi City)|Q11589481|WD=Q11589481|lt=Douzan Jinguji Temple|1=Jingū-ji}}''' : Adjacent. Our company {{ill|lt=Bettoji Temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} before {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. === Cultural assets === === Chiba Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} - Designated on March 30, 2004. === Cultural property designated by Funabashi City === * Shrine building (tangible cultural property (building)) - Designated on February 27, 1992. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saito Sonojo|1=UNKNOWN}} etc. dedicated haiku plaque (tangible cultural property (historical material)) - Designated on October 23, 1980. * Ninomiya Shrine Kagura (Intangible Folk Cultural Property) - Designated on June 26, 1995. * Ginkgo at Ninomiya Shrine (natural monument) - Designated on March 30, 2012. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma Station|Q801534|WD=Q801534|1=Tsudanuma Station}} ({{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|lt=Sobu Main Line|Q1191364|WD=Q1191364|1=Sōbu Main Line}}) From the north exit ** Take the {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya Shrine" (ride time: 20 minutes) ** Take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya-jinja-mae" (20 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Yakuendai Station|Q8047226|WD=Q8047226|1=Yakuendai Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Matsudo Line|Q3553734|WD=Q3553734|1=Keisei Matsudo Line}}) ** Walk (3 minutes) from the Yakuendai Station Iriguchi bus stop on National Route 296, take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya-jinja-mae (10 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Keisei Okubo Station|Q6383851|WD=Q6383851|1=Keisei Ōkubo Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Main Line|Q1063675|WD=Q1063675|1=Keisei Main Line}}) ** Walk (7 minutes) from the Toho Daigaku-mae bus stop, take {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya Shrine (ride time: 6 minutes) *: Keisei buses near Keisei Okubo Station have different routes for going down and going up. The nearest bus stop for going down is "Toho Daigaku-mae" and the nearest bus stop for going up is "Okubo Jujiro". === Source === {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * "Japanese Historical Place Name System Chiba Prefecture Place Names" ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}) Funabashi City Ninomiya Shrine Section * “Meiji, Taisho, and Showa eras preserved in postcard photographs” (Funabashi City Local Museum, March 23, 2005) * “Celebrating the 70th Anniversary of the Municipal System - Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810 years|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}}-{{ill|lt=824 years|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}) |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Gongenzo|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} |Other names = Samukawa Shrine, Miyama Myojin, Ninomiya Myojin|Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} |Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Shimosa Sanzan Seven Year Festival|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Ninomiya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Torii]]'''Ninomiya Shrine'' (Ninomiya Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} Miyama. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, tell them {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In ancient times, it was also called ``Miyama Myojin'' or ``Ninomiya Myojin''. . === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is listed according to the shrine history book. * '''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Kenhaya Susano no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Kushinada Hime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushiinada Hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira Mikoto|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}''' :According to legend, when {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Moritsune|Q11623227|WD=Q11623227|1=Fujiwara no Moritsune}}, who was demoted in 4th year ({{ill|lt=1180|Q19702|WD=Q19702|1=1180}}), settled in this area, he enshrined his ancestor, Fujiwara Tokihira, in his side hall. Since Tokihira is a political enemy of {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (Tenjin), it is said that the parishioners of Tenjin did not visit the shrine . * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Daisaku no Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}}''' :{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, a nearby mukakusha, was {{ill|lt=together enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondabetsu no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}''' :In the Meiji period, Setsusha and Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. === History === According to the shrine's legend, the shrine history book was founded by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} during the {{ill|lt=Konin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}} to {{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}). . Like place names, the name ``Miyama'' appeared to be ``Miyama'' in ancient times, and was attributed to our company. {{ill|lt=Miyama clan|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Miyama clan}} served as {{ill|lt=priests|Q11589454|WD=Q11589454|1=Shinkan (official)}} for generations. ``Chiba Prefecture place name'' Miyama Village. .``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} states: Samukawa Shrine", but our company is said to be {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. It is also said that it was revered by the imperial court as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} (however, {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}} is also called Ninomiya). It is unknown when it began to be called Ninomiya Shrine, but (Japan)|Q1327472|Inuigen}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1303|Q5614|WD=Q5614|1=1303}}) The temple bell (stored in Shohoin, Narita City) says ``Soshu Ninomiya Shadan'', which suggests that this name already existed in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} In 19 years ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} gave {{ill|lt=shuinji/black seals|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinji|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} 10 koku and , {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}The shogun made donations many times. {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}}It is introduced in ``Narita Pilgrimage Record'' published in 1858 along with an illustration of ``Miyama Myo Shrine''. After entering {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In 1910, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Gensetsusha Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. Since ancient times, "Shirenshita" (area of parishioners) has consisted of 21.3 villages (currently {{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} eastern and northern areas, {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}, {{ja link|Yachiyo City|Q328651}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}), and its influence can still be seen today at annual festivals. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The current shrine building was rebuilt during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} years ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|WD=Q7690|1=1772}} to {{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|WD=Q7719|1=1781}}). Both the main shrine and worship hall have copper plate roofs, but the main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}11 ({{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}})1 In October, the worship hall had its {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531|1=Thatching}} roof replaced in Taisho 14 ({{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}}). The floor plan of this shrine is {{ill|lt=Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}}, which was popular during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but the floor height from {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} to {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is the same. In addition, the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q11637472|WD=Q11637472|1=eaves}}'s {{ill|lt=kumimono|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} and koshigumi were designed and constructed using the regular {{ill|lt=miya carpenter|Q65261787|WD=Q65261787|1=miyadaiku}} method of the time. In 1978 ({{ill|lt=1978|Q2483|WD=Q2483|1=1978}}) {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, it was designated as {{ill|lt=List of Funabashi City Designated Cultural Properties|Q11614137|WD=Q11614137|lt=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties|1=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties}}. File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) haiden.JPG|Shrine of worshipFile:ninomiyajinnjya7.jpg|Mae Square File: Ninomiya.jpg|Photo of the shrine building in 1918 === Approach === [[File:Ninomiya-Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Sando]] When you approach the shrine from the torii gate in front of the shrine, the approach becomes lower and then crosses the valley. Furthermore, a stream flows at the bottom of the valley, and the water vein is said to lead to a pond within the grounds of {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}}, which is enshrined in {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma|Q7849928|WD=Q7849928|1=Tsudanuma}} in {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}. If you walk along the valley floor in a straight line, by the time you reach the top of the stone steps, you will be able to see the Karahafu facing the worship hall, and the master carpenter's {{ill|lt=Design|Q82604|WD=Q82604|1=Design}} has been ingenious so that you can take your time to admire the large, majestic {{ill|lt=Karagohafu|Q977293|WD=Q977293|1=Karahafu}} curves and the majestic carvings. === Festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} (January 1st) * Oshahatsu Festival (January 7th) * Otsutsu porridge cooking ritual (January 14th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Festival|1=Setsubun}} (February 3rd) * '''Regular Festival''' (October 16th) * '''Daikaage'' (Obiyo, {{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}night of November 13th) :A festival named after the bonfires lit by villagers to welcome Fujiwara no Moritsune and others who were said to have been exiled to this area. * '''{{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}}''' (Year of the Ox/Year of the Wild) :A festival held once every seven years, centered around our company, with nine shrines in Funabashi City, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yachiyo City|Q328651|WD=Q328651|1=Yachiyo, Chiba}}, and Narashino City participating. Our company plays the role of "father." === Surroundings === * '''{{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple (Funabashi City)|Q11589481|WD=Q11589481|lt=Douzan Jinguji Temple|1=Jingū-ji}}''' : Adjacent. Our company {{ill|lt=Bettoji Temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} before {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. === Cultural assets === === Chiba Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} - Designated on March 30, 2004. === Cultural property designated by Funabashi City === * Shrine building (tangible cultural property (building)) - Designated on February 27, 1992. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saito Sonojo|1=UNKNOWN}} etc. dedicated haiku plaque (tangible cultural property (historical material)) - Designated on October 23, 1980. * Ninomiya Shrine Kagura (Intangible Folk Cultural Property) - Designated on June 26, 1995. * Ginkgo at Ninomiya Shrine (natural monument) - Designated on March 30, 2012. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma Station|Q801534|WD=Q801534|1=Tsudanuma Station}} ({{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|lt=Sobu Main Line|Q1191364|WD=Q1191364|1=Sōbu Main Line}}) From the north exit ** Take the {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya Shrine" (ride time: 20 minutes) ** Take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya-jinja-mae" (20 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Yakuendai Station|Q8047226|WD=Q8047226|1=Yakuendai Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Matsudo Line|Q3553734|WD=Q3553734|1=Keisei Matsudo Line}}) ** Walk (3 minutes) from the Yakuendai Station Iriguchi bus stop on National Route 296, take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya-jinja-mae (10 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Keisei Okubo Station|Q6383851|WD=Q6383851|1=Keisei Ōkubo Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Main Line|Q1063675|WD=Q1063675|1=Keisei Main Line}}) ** Walk (7 minutes) from the Toho Daigaku-mae bus stop, take {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya Shrine (ride time: 6 minutes) *: Keisei buses near Keisei Okubo Station have different routes for going down and going up. The nearest bus stop for going down is "Toho Daigaku-mae" and the nearest bus stop for going up is "Okubo Jujiro". === Source === {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * "Japanese Historical Place Name System Chiba Prefecture Place Names" ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}) Funabashi City Ninomiya Shrine Section * “Meiji, Taisho, and Showa eras preserved in postcard photographs” (Funabashi City Local Museum, March 23, 2005) * “Celebrating the 70th Anniversary of the Municipal System - 100 Years of Funabashi” (Kyoto Publishing, February 5, 2007) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Mr. Miyama|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Mr. Miyama}} * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} * {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Samukawa Shrine (Chiba City)|Q11457393|WD=Q11457393|lt=Samukawa Shrine|1=Samugawa Shrine}} (Chiba Prefecture {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}{{ill|lt=Chuo Ward (Chiba City)|Q1075869|WD=Q1075869|lt=Chuo Ward|1=Chūō-ku, Chiba}}) - Like our company, Shikinai Shrine and Samukawa Shrine Ronsha * {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} (Chiba Prefecture {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}}) - Another shrine named Shimousa Province Ninomiya * {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} === External link === {{commonscat|Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi)}} * [http://www.ninomiya-jinja.jp Ninomiya Shrine] (official website) {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ninomiya Shinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:船橋市の神社]] [[Category:下総国の式内社]] [[Category:千葉県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下総国|社2]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:嵯峨天皇]] [[Category:藤原時平]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:QNinomiya Shrine (Q11371511)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QNinomiya Shrine (Q11371511)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)}}[[Category:qq]]

Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810 years|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}}-{{ill|lt=824 years|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}) |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Gongenzo|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} |Other names = Samukawa Shrine, Miyama Myojin, Ninomiya Myojin|Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 16th|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}} |Shinto ritual = {{ill|lt=Shimosa Sanzan Seven Year Festival|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Ninomiya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Torii]]'''Ninomiya Shrine'' (Ninomiya Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} Miyama. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, tell them {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. In ancient times, it was also called ``Miyama Myojin'' or ``Ninomiya Myojin''. . === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is listed according to the shrine history book. * '''{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Kenhaya Susano no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Kushinada Hime|Q505469|WD=Q505469|lt=Kushiinada Hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' * '''{{ill|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira Mikoto|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}''' :According to legend, when {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Moritsune|Q11623227|WD=Q11623227|1=Fujiwara no Moritsune}}, who was demoted in 4th year ({{ill|lt=1180|Q19702|WD=Q19702|1=1180}}), settled in this area, he enshrined his ancestor, Fujiwara Tokihira, in his side hall. Since Tokihira is a political enemy of {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (Tenjin), it is said that the parishioners of Tenjin did not visit the shrine . * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Daisaku no Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}}''' :{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}} Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, a nearby mukakusha, was {{ill|lt=together enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondabetsu no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}''' :In the Meiji period, Setsusha and Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. === History === According to the shrine's legend, the shrine history book was founded by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Saga|Q316654|WD=Q316654|1=Emperor Saga}} during the {{ill|lt=Konin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=810|Q23819|WD=Q23819|1=810}} to {{ill|lt=824|Q30353|WD=Q30353|1=824}}). . Like place names, the name ``Miyama'' appeared to be ``Miyama'' in ancient times, and was attributed to our company. {{ill|lt=Miyama clan|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Miyama clan}} served as {{ill|lt=priests|Q11589454|WD=Q11589454|1=Shinkan (official)}} for generations. ``Chiba Prefecture place name'' Miyama Village. .``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} states: Samukawa Shrine", but our company is said to be {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. It is also said that it was revered by the imperial court as {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Shimosa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} (however, {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}} is also called Ninomiya). It is unknown when it began to be called Ninomiya Shrine, but (Japan)|Q1327472|Inuigen}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1303|Q5614|WD=Q5614|1=1303}}) The temple bell (stored in Shohoin, Narita City) says ``Soshu Ninomiya Shadan'', which suggests that this name already existed in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} In 19 years ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} gave {{ill|lt=shuinji/black seals|Q11521520|WD=Q11521520|lt=shuinji|1=shuinchi and kokuinchi}} 10 koku and , {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|1=Tokugawa shogunate}}The shogun made donations many times. {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}}It is introduced in ``Narita Pilgrimage Record'' published in 1858 along with an illustration of ``Miyama Myo Shrine''. After entering {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In 1910, Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Gensetsusha Awa Hachiman Shrine were enshrined together. Since ancient times, "Shirenshita" (area of parishioners) has consisted of 21.3 villages (currently {{ill|lt=Funabashi City|Q271417|WD=Q271417|1=Funabashi}} eastern and northern areas, {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}, {{ja link|Yachiyo City|Q328651}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}), and its influence can still be seen today at annual festivals. === Precincts === === Shrine building === The current shrine building was rebuilt during the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} years ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|WD=Q7690|1=1772}} to {{ill|lt=1781|Q7719|WD=Q7719|1=1781}}). Both the main shrine and worship hall have copper plate roofs, but the main shrine was built in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}11 ({{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}})1 In October, the worship hall had its {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531|1=Thatching}} roof replaced in Taisho 14 ({{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}}). The floor plan of this shrine is {{ill|lt=Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}}, which was popular during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, but the floor height from {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} to {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} is the same. In addition, the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q11637472|WD=Q11637472|1=eaves}}'s {{ill|lt=kumimono|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} and koshigumi were designed and constructed using the regular {{ill|lt=miya carpenter|Q65261787|WD=Q65261787|1=miyadaiku}} method of the time. In 1978 ({{ill|lt=1978|Q2483|WD=Q2483|1=1978}}) {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, it was designated as {{ill|lt=List of Funabashi City Designated Cultural Properties|Q11614137|WD=Q11614137|lt=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties|1=Funabashi City's Tangible Cultural Properties}}. File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi) haiden.JPG|Shrine of worshipFile:ninomiyajinnjya7.jpg|Mae Square File: Ninomiya.jpg|Photo of the shrine building in 1918 === Approach === [[File:Ninomiya-Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Sando]] When you approach the shrine from the torii gate in front of the shrine, the approach becomes lower and then crosses the valley. Furthermore, a stream flows at the bottom of the valley, and the water vein is said to lead to a pond within the grounds of {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}}, which is enshrined in {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma|Q7849928|WD=Q7849928|1=Tsudanuma}} in {{ill|lt=Narashino City|Q318047|WD=Q318047|1=Narashino}}. If you walk along the valley floor in a straight line, by the time you reach the top of the stone steps, you will be able to see the Karahafu facing the worship hall, and the master carpenter's {{ill|lt=Design|Q82604|WD=Q82604|1=Design}} has been ingenious so that you can take your time to admire the large, majestic {{ill|lt=Karagohafu|Q977293|WD=Q977293|1=Karahafu}} curves and the majestic carvings. === Festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824|1=Saitan-sai}} (January 1st) * Oshahatsu Festival (January 7th) * Otsutsu porridge cooking ritual (January 14th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|WD=Q746798|lt=Setsubun Festival|1=Setsubun}} (February 3rd) * '''Regular Festival''' (October 16th) * '''Daikaage'' (Obiyo, {{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}night of November 13th) :A festival named after the bonfires lit by villagers to welcome Fujiwara no Moritsune and others who were said to have been exiled to this area. * '''{{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}}''' (Year of the Ox/Year of the Wild) :A festival held once every seven years, centered around our company, with nine shrines in Funabashi City, {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Yachiyo City|Q328651|WD=Q328651|1=Yachiyo, Chiba}}, and Narashino City participating. Our company plays the role of "father." === Surroundings === * '''{{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple (Funabashi City)|Q11589481|WD=Q11589481|lt=Douzan Jinguji Temple|1=Jingū-ji}}''' : Adjacent. Our company {{ill|lt=Bettoji Temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} before {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. === Cultural assets === === Chiba Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} - Designated on March 30, 2004. === Cultural property designated by Funabashi City === * Shrine building (tangible cultural property (building)) - Designated on February 27, 1992. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Saito Sonojo|1=UNKNOWN}} etc. dedicated haiku plaque (tangible cultural property (historical material)) - Designated on October 23, 1980. * Ninomiya Shrine Kagura (Intangible Folk Cultural Property) - Designated on June 26, 1995. * Ginkgo at Ninomiya Shrine (natural monument) - Designated on March 30, 2012. === Transportation access === * {{ill|lt=Tsudanuma Station|Q801534|WD=Q801534|1=Tsudanuma Station}} ({{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{ill|lt=Sobu Main Line|Q1191364|WD=Q1191364|1=Sōbu Main Line}}) From the north exit ** Take the {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya Shrine" (ride time: 20 minutes) ** Take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, "Ninomiya-jinja-mae" (20 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Yakuendai Station|Q8047226|WD=Q8047226|1=Yakuendai Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Matsudo Line|Q3553734|WD=Q3553734|1=Keisei Matsudo Line}}) ** Walk (3 minutes) from the Yakuendai Station Iriguchi bus stop on National Route 296, take the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Funabashi Shinkeisei Bus|1=UNKNOWN}} (Tsu 08 bound for Ninomiya Jinja-mae) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya-jinja-mae (10 minute ride) * From {{ill|lt=Keisei Okubo Station|Q6383851|WD=Q6383851|1=Keisei Ōkubo Station}} ({{ill|lt=Keisei Main Line|Q1063675|WD=Q1063675|1=Keisei Main Line}}) ** Walk (7 minutes) from the Toho Daigaku-mae bus stop, take {{ill|lt=Keisei Bus|Q11374367|WD=Q11374367|1=Keisei Bus}} (Tsu 02 bound for Ninomiya Shrine) and get off at the final stop, Ninomiya Shrine (ride time: 6 minutes) *: Keisei buses near Keisei Okubo Station have different routes for going down and going up. The nearest bus stop for going down is "Toho Daigaku-mae" and the nearest bus stop for going up is "Okubo Jujiro". === Source === {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * "Japanese Historical Place Name System Chiba Prefecture Place Names" ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}) Funabashi City Ninomiya Shrine Section * “Meiji, Taisho, and Showa eras preserved in postcard photographs” (Funabashi City Local Museum, March 23, 2005) * “Celebrating the 70th Anniversary of the Municipal System - 100 Years of Funabashi” (Kyoto Publishing, February 5, 2007) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Mr. Miyama|Q11355183|WD=Q11355183|1=Mr. Miyama}} * {{ill|lt=Seven Year Festival of Shimousa Sanzan|Q11361280|WD=Q11361280|1=Shimōsa Miyama Seven-Year Festival}} * {{ill|lt=Kikuta Shrine|Q11619900|WD=Q11619900|1=Kikuta Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Samukawa Shrine (Chiba City)|Q11457393|WD=Q11457393|lt=Samukawa Shrine|1=Samugawa Shrine}} (Chiba Prefecture {{ill|lt=Chiba City|Q170616|WD=Q170616|1=Chiba (city)}}{{ill|lt=Chuo Ward (Chiba City)|Q1075869|WD=Q1075869|lt=Chuo Ward|1=Chūō-ku, Chiba}}) - Like our company, Shikinai Shrine and Samukawa Shrine Ronsha * {{ill|lt=Tamazaki Shrine|Q11572538|WD=Q11572538|1=Tamasaki Shrine}} (Chiba Prefecture {{ill|lt=Asahi City|Q637868|WD=Q637868|1=Asahi, Chiba}}) - Another shrine named Shimousa Province Ninomiya * {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} === External link === {{commonscat|Ninomiya-jinja (Funabashi)}} * [http://www.ninomiya-jinja.jp Ninomiya Shrine] (official website) {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Ninomiya Shrine (Q11371511)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:船橋市の神社]] [[Category:下総国の式内社]] [[Category:千葉県の旧郷社]] [[Category:下総国|社2]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:嵯峨天皇]] [[Category:藤原時平]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]]
